coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence immunoassay based on grapefruit peel-extracted carbon quantum dots encapsulated into silica nanospheres for p53 protein
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Pan
Ke, Yue
Lu, Jiong
Huang, Zhengru
Zhu, Xiaofeng
Wei, Bin
Huang, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: We herein designed a simple and feasible photoluminescence (PL) immunoassay for sensitive detection of p53 protein in biological fluid by using grapefruit peel-extracted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal-generation tags. CQDs were first synthesized on the basis of grapefruit peel as the carbon source via a typical hydrothermal method. Thereafter, the synthesized CQDs were encapsulated into silica nanoparticles (CQD-SiNP) for the signal enhancement. In the presence of p53, a sandwich-type immunoreaction was fulfilled on monoclonal anti-p53 capture antibody-coated microplate by using CQD-SiNP-labeled polyclonal anti-p53 secondary antibody. Relative to CQD-labeled strategy, use of CQD-SiNP could amplify the photoluminescence signal toward the same-concentration target p53. Under the optimum conditions, the photoluminescence intensity via the CQD-SiNP-labeled strategy exhibited high sensitivity, and allowed the detection of target p53 as low as a concentration of 2.7 pg mL−1. No interfering signals were acquired toward non-target analytes including biomarkers or enzymes in human serum. Below 15% of relative standard deviations (RSDs) were obtained for detection of p53 analyte in all cases. Additionally, CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassays were utilized for the evaluation of real human serum samples, and received well-matched results with commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence immunoassay
Protein 53
Grapefruit peel-extracted carbon quantum dots
Carbon dots-encapsulated silica nanoparticles
Tumor markers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Labeling of CQD-SiNP with polyclonal anti-p53 antibody (CQD-SiNP-pAb2)
Feasible evaluation and control tests of CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassay
Calibration plots of photoluminescence immunoassay toward p53 standards
Specificity and stability of CQD-SiNP-pAb2-based photoluminescence immunoassay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Training Project for Scientific Research Talents , Fujian Provincial Health and Family Planning Commission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Startup Fund for Scientific Research , Fujian Medical University (Grant no.: 2017XQ1064 ), Training Project for Scientific Research Talents , Fujian Provincial Health and Family Planning Commission (Grant no.: 2018-1-53 ) and Research Project on Education and Teaching Reform , Fujian Medical University (Grant no.: Y17007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sandwich-type photoluminescence (PL) immunoassay toward target p53 protein on mouse monoclonal anti-p53 antibody (mAb1)-coated microplate by using rabbit polyclonal anti-p53 antibody (pAb2)-labeled carbon quantum dots-encapsulated silica nanoparticles (CQD-SiNP) (note: CQDs were exacted from grapefruit peel by using hydrothermal method).

(A,B) TEM images of (A) CQDs exacted from grapefruit peel (insets: HRTEM image and fluorescence photograph) and (B) CQD-SiNP (inset: fluorescence photograph), and (C) XRD pattern and (D) XPS spectrum of CQDs.
(A) FTIR spectra, (B) zeta potential profiles and (C) DLS date of (a) CQD-SiNP, (b) GOPS/CQD-SiNP and (c) pAb2/GOPS/CQD-SiNP.

(A) PL emission spectra of (a) mAb1-modified microplate, (b) probe 'a' + 0.05 ng mL−1 p53, (c) probe 'b' + pAb2-CQD-SiNP and (d) probe 'a' + pAb2-CQD-SiNP; (B) PL emission spectra of (a) mAb1-modified microplate, (b) probe 'a' + 0.05 ng mL−1 p53 + pAb2-CQD and (c) probe 'a' + 0.05 ng mL−1 p53 + pAb2-CQD-SiNP; (C,D) effects of (c) CQD mass sin 30-mL AOT isooctane (0.1 M) suspension including 2.5-mL TEOS for preparation of CQD-SiNP and (d) pAb2 volume in the CQD-SiNP aqueous solution (1.0 mL, 10 mg mL-1) for the preparation of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 on the photoluminescence immunoassay (0.05 ng mL-1 used in this case).

(A) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CQD-SiNP-based immunoassay for the known p53 standards (inset: the corresponding calibration plots); (B) the specificity of PL immunoassay against 1.0 ng mL−1 target p53, neuron-specific enolase (NSE; 100 ng mL−1), lactate acid dehydrogenase (LAD; 100 ng mL−1), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP; 100 ng mL−1), cancer antigen 125 (CA 125; 100 ng mL−1), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA; 100 ng mL−1) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA; 100 ng mL−1); and (C) the stability of the as-prepared pAb2-CQD-SiNP within the one-year storage by assaying 1.0 ng mL-1 p53 intermittently.
Comparison of analytical properties of photoluminescence immunoassay toward target p53 with other detection schemes.
Comparison of the results obtained by the photoluminescence immunoassay and commercial human p53 ELISA kit (65 pg mL−1; 0.23–15 ng mL−1; Abcam, ab171571) for human serum specimens.

row.entry: method; concentration [ng mL−1, mean ± SD (RSD), n = 3] a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The high-concentration samples were calculated on the basis of the dilution ratio.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence immunoassay based on grapefruit peel-extracted carbon quantum dots encapsulated into silica nanospheres for p53 protein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, Changshu No.2 People's Hospital, Changshu 215500, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Xiangyang Central Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein designed a simple and feasible photoluminescence (PL) immunoassay for sensitive detection of p53 protein in biological fluid by using grapefruit peel-extracted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the signal-generation tags. CQDs were first synthesized on the basis of grapefruit peel as the carbon source via a typical hydrothermal method. Thereafter, the synthesized CQDs were encapsulated into silica nanoparticles (CQD-SiNP) for the signal enhancement. In the presence of p53, a sandwich-type immunoreaction was fulfilled on monoclonal anti-p53 capture antibody-coated microplate by using CQD-SiNP-labeled polyclonal anti-p53 secondary antibody. Relative to CQD-labeled strategy, use of CQD-SiNP could amplify the photoluminescence signal toward the same-concentration target p53. Under the optimum conditions, the photoluminescence intensity via the CQD-SiNP-labeled strategy exhibited high sensitivity, and allowed the detection of target p53 as low as a concentration of 2.7 pg mL−1. No interfering signals were acquired toward non-target analytes including biomarkers or enzymes in human serum. Below 15% of relative standard deviations (RSDs) were obtained for detection of p53 analyte in all cases. Additionally, CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassays were utilized for the evaluation of real human serum samples, and received well-matched results with commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, one of the most threatening disease groups, involves abnormal cell growth with the potential to invade or spread to other parts of the body [1]. Cancer markers including serum tumor markers and potential prognostic factors for tumors, e.g., alpha-fetoprotein, prostate-specific antigen and neuron-specific enolase, play very important roles in clinical diagnosis for evaluating therapy and detecting recurrence toward patients with certain tumor-associated diseases [2,3]. Typically, cancer markers can be determined in serum or rarely in urine or other body fluids, often by immunoassay but other techniques such as enzyme activity assay are sometimes used [4,5]. In contrast, microscopic visualization in tissue by immunohistochemistry does not give quantitative results. In this regard, the immunological methods have become the predominant analytical techniques for the quantitative determination of cancer biomarkers [6,7].

Recently, various immunoassays have been reported for evaluating cancer biomarkers on the basis of the different signal-generation mechanisms, e.g., (photo)electrochemistry, quartz crystal microbalance, (electro)chemiluminescence, surface plasmon resonance and fluorescence [8–13]. Among these strategies, photoluminescence (PL) immunosensors have mainly been considered for the immunoassays because of their overwhelming advantages over applying visible radiation, non-destructive operation, and rapid signal generation/readout [14]. Naturally, the sensitivity and analytical properties of PL immunoassays are dependent on an in-depth design of molecular tags such as fluorophores, and emerging techniques in this field will inevitably play an important role in future advances [15–17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an interesting class of carbon nanomaterials, have garnered great attention as a potential competitor to traditional semiconductor quantum dots because of their unique optical and electronic properties with biocompatibility and strong luminescence showing bright promise for integration into the devices of photovoltaic and light emitting applications in the chemical sensing, biosensing, nanomedicine, bioimaging, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [18–20]. Isnaeni et al. studied photoluminescence of CQDs from ginger and galangal herbs by using microwave technique [21]. Li et al. designed a new photoluminescence sensing system for sensitive and selective to bromate anions based on carbon dots [22]. Hu et al. prepared dual photoluminescence centers from inorganic-salt-functionalized CQDs for ratiometric pH sensing [23]. Typically, the luminescence properties are dependent on the core and surface state emission [24]. Momper et al. constructed enhanced PL properties of a carbon dot system through surface interaction with polymeric nanoparticles [25]. Zhang et al. reported a facile hydrothermal approach to adjust the photoluminescence peaks of CQDs from blue, green to orange by controlling the surface passivation reaction during the synthesis [26]. Recently, we found that the source of CQDs would also affect the quantum yield of the photoluminescence. Basu et al. synthesized photoluminescent CQDs with 4–5 nm in diameter from starch (Tapioca Sago) for fluoride ion sensing via a solution method under mild conditions [27]. Li et al. reported a new one-step hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CQDs by using grape skin as raw material [28]. The obtained CQDs had excellent solubility, oxidation resistance, stability and high quantum yield. To this end, our motivation of this work is to extract the low-cost carbon dots from the local grapefruit peel for the development of photoluminescence immunoassay.

Protein 53 (p53), a tumor suppressor protein in human encoded by the TP53 gene, is crucial in the multicellular organisms involving in preventing cancer such as oral and ocular squamous cell carcinoma [29]. Typically, p53 has many mechanisms of anticancer function and plays a role in apoptosis, genic stability, and inhibition of angiogenesis [30]. Meanwhile, p53 becomes activated in response to myriad stressors, including but not limited to DNA damage, oxidative stress, osmotic shock, ribonucleotide depletion, and deregulated oncogene expression [31]. Thus, the sensitive detection of p53 in biological fluids is of great importance of point-of-care diagnostic. Herein we report on a simple low-cost method for synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by using grapefruit peel as the carbon source, which is utilized for the photoluminescence detection of p53 with a competitive Immunoassay format (Scheme 1 ). To amplify the detectable signal, the synthesized CQDs are entrapped into silica nanoparticles, which are used for the labeling of polyclonal anti-p53 detection antibody. The immunoreaction is carried out in monoclonal anti-p53 capture antibody-coated microplate. In the presence of target p53, the sandwiched immunocomplex is formed accompanying introduction of CQDs-entrapped silica nanoparticles in the microplate. CQDs-encapsulated silica nanoparticles can enhance the photoluminescence intensity relative to CQDs alone. This study aims to construct a photoluminescence immunoassay for the screening of low-abundance p53.

Mouse monoclonal [PAb 240] anti-p53 antibody (mAb1; unconjugated, ab26) and polyclonal rabbit anti-p53 antibody (pAb2; unconjugated, ab131442) and human p53 ELISA kits including different-concentration p53 standards (serum, plasma and cell culture supernatant; sandwich; linear range: 0.1563–10 ng mL−1; LOD: 44 pg mL−1) were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai, China). High-binding 96-well polystyrene microplates were achieved from Greiner Bio-One (Frickenhausen, Germany). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96%–99%), (3-glycidyloxypropyl) trimethoxysilane (C9H20O5Si, GOPS) and tetramethoxysilane (TEOS) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO 63103, USA). Cyclohexane, n-hexanol, and ammonium heydroxide (25 wt %) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). All other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water used in this work was provide from a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q, Germany). Phosphate-buffered saline (0.01 M, PBS) solutions with different pH values containing 0.15 M NaCl were prepared by mixing 0.01 M K2HPO4 and 0.01 M KH2PO4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from grapefruit peel (brought from the local supermarket) by a simple convenient one-step hydrothermal method. Prior to synthesis, the grapefruit peel was initially cut into small pieces, and then cleaned by ultrapure water and dried at 60 °C. Following that, 1.0 g of bulk grapefruit peel was milled with 50-mL ultrapure water by using a Midea food mixer (BP252 AG, Guangdong, China), and the as-prepared suspension was filtered through cotton, followed by Whatman filter paper (Whatman grade 40, thickness 210 μm and pore size 8.0 μm). Afterwards, 25 mL of the extract was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered with membrane filter paper to remove the larger or agglomerated carbon nanoparticles, and the supernatant fraction was centrifuged for 15 min at 15,000g. Dialysis was undertaken with a MW 1000 dialysis bag for 24 h and freeze-dried. Finally, the obtained CQDs were dispersed into ultrapure water for further use.

CQDs-encapsulated silica nanoparticles were prepared at room temperature by using a reverse micelle method similar to the literature [32]. Prior to synthesis, a 30-mL AOT isooctane (0.1 M) suspension including 50-mg CQDs and 2.5-mL TEOS was prepared under vigorous stirring. After that, 1.8 mL of NH3·H2O (25 wt %) was slowly dropped into the resulting mixture until formation of translucent CQD-SiNP colloids. Following that, absolute ethanol (3.0 mL) was added and stirred for 10 min to breakdown the reverse micelles with the formation of two immiscible layers of aqueous ethanol and isooctane. The ethanol was carefully removed by using a separating funnel, and the obtained nanocomposites were washed with isooctane and centrifuged (10 min, 10,000g) to remove excess AOT. The product were dispersed into 10-mL ultrapure water by vigorous stirring for 30 min and the dispersed system was dialysed against ultrapure water for 2 h using a 12 kDa cut-off dialysis bag to remove small CQDs and pure silica nanoparticles. The obtained CQD-SiNP nanocomposites were used for the labeling of polyclonal anti-p53 antibody.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labeling of CQD-SiNP with polyclonal anti-p53 antibody (CQD-SiNP-pAb2)
ce:section.ce:para: Conjugation of CQD-SiNPs with polyclonal anti-p53 antibody was prepared by coupling with GOPS cross-linkage referring to this work [33]. Initially 50 mg of dried CQD-SiNPs in the vacuum at 80 °C was added into GOPS (5.0 mL, 5%, v/v) in dry toluene, and reacted under slight stirring for 6 h at room temperature. Following that, the suspension was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000g and washed with toluene/ethanol mixture for three times. The precipitate was dried and activated in an oven at 80 °C for 60 min under nitrogen atmosphere. Afterward, polyclonal anti-p53 antibody (100 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1) was added into the CQD-SiNP aqueous solution (1.0 mL, 10 mg mL−1), and shaken on a shaker for 6 h at 4 °C to react the epoxy group of GOPS with surface accessible amines of the antibody. The as-prepared suspension was then centrifuged and washed as before, and dispersed in PBS (1.0 mL, 10 mM, pH 7.4) for use.

Scheme 1 gives the schematic illustration of photoluminescence immunoassay toward target p53 with a sandwich assay format based on CQD-SiNP-labeled signal probe. The immunoreaction was firstly carried out in antibody-coated microplate. Prior to measurement, a high-binding polystyrene 96-well microplate was coated overnight at 4 °C with 50 μL per well of monoclonal mouse anti-p53 antibody (mAb1) at a concentration of 10 μg mL−1 in 0.05 M sodium carbonate buffer (pH 9.6). The microplate was covered with adhesive plastics plate sealing film to prevent evaporation. On the following day, the microplate was washed three times with the washing buffer (10 mM PBS + 0.05% Tween 20, v/v; pH 7.4), and then incubated with 300 μL per well of the blocking buffer (10 mM PBS + 1.0 wt % BSA; pH 7.4) for 60 min at 37 °C with shaking. The microplate was then washed as before. Following that, 50 μL of standards of known p53 concentrations, control specimens or unknowns was added into the microplate, and incubated for 60 min at 37 °C under shaking. After washing, 50 μL of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 solution (10 mg mL−1) was added into the well and incubated for 60 min under the same conditions. The plates were washed three times with washing buffer and one time with water. Finally, the photoluminescence spectra were recorded in PBS (300 μL, 10 mM, pH 7.4) on an Infinite® M1000 Pro multimode reader (Tecan Trading AG, Switzerland) with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (λex = 340 nm; λem = 425 nm). The PL intensity was collected and registered as the photoluminescence immunoassay relative to target p53 activity. All incubations and measurements were conducted at room temperature. All data were calculated in triplicate unless otherwise specifically stated.

For the successful development of high-efficient photoluminescence immunoassay, a simple and low-cost detection platform would be advantageous in the practical applications, especially for the developing countries and remote areas. In this work, the signal-generation tags are easily achieved from the local grapefruit peel, whereas the PL assay is executed in a low-cost microplate. Recently, molecular tags such as fluorophores are increasing being replaced by nanoparticles, e.g., quantum dots, since they tend to have superior optical properties, substantially greater chemical stability, and stability against photobleaching. Unfavorably, the signal of using quantum dots alone is relatively weak because of the limitation of emission intensity. The signal amplification is usually fulfilled by using quantum dots-encapsulated polymers or microspheres. Nanosized silica particles have been investigated and proved to be an ideal host since they are highly chemically and thermally stable with a large loading capacity, a fine suspendability in aqueous solution and relatively environmental inert. CQD-loaded silica nanoparticles are expected to enhance the sensitivity of photoluminescence immunoassay.

To realize our design, quantum dots before and after encapsulation with silica nanoparticles are characterized by using high-solution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-synthesized CQDs could be homogeneously dispersed into the ultrapure water, and the average size was very small with 4.2 ± 0.11 nm in diameter. Also, we could obviously observe the crystal lattice spacing distances (∼0.24 nm) of CQDs (Fig. 1A, top inset), which was almost the same as the previous reports [34–36]. Fig. 1B gives typical TEM image of CQD-SiNPs with the size of ∼16 nm in diameter. Moreover, almost no small SiNPs and CQDs appeared in the suspension, indicating that they were removed during the filter and dialysis processes. Furthermore, the photoluminescence signal was still strong, as showed from the corresponding fluorescence photographs (Fig. 1A-B, insets), which provided a precondition for the development of CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassay.

Logically, one question arises to whether the extracted nanostructures from grapefruit peel were real carbon quantum dots. To verify this concern, we used X-ray diffraction (XRD; Shimadzu, X-6000, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation to investigate the pattern of the nanoscales. As shown in Fig. 1C, several characteristic peaks at 11.9°, 20.8°, 23.1°, 42.7° and 45.1 (2θ) were acquired at this pattern. A weak broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.1° corresponded to (002) crystal-lographic reflection of the hexagonal bulk-graphite with highly disordered carbon atoms [37]. The reason for the low intensity and broadness for XRD patterns of the CQDs was attributed to the small crystallite size as well as resulting stress and strain effect, and the rich surface oxygen groups of CQDs [38]. The strong peaks at 11.9° and 20.8° were corresponded to the (200) and (311) planes of carbon, whilst the weak peaks at 42.7° and 45.1° derived from the plane of (533) and (711) [39]. In addition, the surface composition of the nanostructures was monitored through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a VG Scientific Escalab 250 spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray (1486.6 eV) (Thermo-VG Sci. Inc., USA) (Fig. 1D). Two characteristic peaks at 287 eV and 534 eV were achieved at the XPS survey spectra, which corresponded to the K-shell peaks of C1s and O1s, respectively. The oxygen-containing element on the surface mainly originated from the oxidation states with carbonyl groups or hydroxyl functional groups [40]. These results revealed that carbon quantum dots could be successfully synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method from grapefruit peel.

As mentioned above, the antibody was labeled onto the CQD-SiNP by the cross-linkage GOPS. It is important to verify that polyclonal anti-p53 antibody could be conjugated onto the CQD-SiNP. To clarify this point, fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to monitor the CQD-SiNPs before and after modification with GOPS and antibody in sequence (Fig. 2 A). Curve 'a' shows the FTIR spectrum of CQD-SiNPs alone. Upon conjugation of GOPS with CQD-SiNPs, an obvious characteristic peak at 914 cm−1 was observed, which was assigned to the epoxy group of GOPS [41]. When GOPS-modified CQD-SiNP reacted with the antibody, favorably, two absorption peaks at 1671 and 1576 cm−1 were achieved (curve 'c'), which stemmed from the secondary structure of the protein (antibody): 1671 cm−1 for amide I groups corresponding to the CO stretching vibration of peptide linkages and 1576 cm−1 for the amide II groups from a combination of NH bending with CN stretching [42]. Also, the zeta potentials of CQD-SiNP after modification with GOPS and pAb2 antibody were studied by microelectrophoresis (Apparatus Mk II, Rank Brothers Ltd, England). As seen from Fig. 2B, the zeta potentials were -11.7 mV for CQD-SiNP, -2.4 mV for GOPS/CQD-SiNP and -23.6 mV for CQD-SiNP-pAb2, respectively. The negative potential (-11.7 mV) for CQD-SiNP was attributed to the hydroxyl group on the surface of silica nanoparticles. Due to the presence of epoxy group on the GOPS, however, the zeta potential of the formed GOPS/CQD-SiNP decreased relative to pure CQD-SiNP. The increasing zeta potential of pAb2 introduction was most likely as a consequence of the result that the isoelectronic point of pAb2 antibody (as a kind of proteins) was less than pH 7.4, which displayed a negatively charged species in pH 7.4 PBS. Importantly, the sizes of CQD-SiNP gradually increased upon conjugation of GOPS and pAb2 antibody in turn from 16.8 ± 2.1 nm to 18.3 ± 2.6 nm to 29.1 ± 1.9 nm, respectively (Fig. 2C). On the basis of these results, we might make an affirmative conclusion on the successful formation of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 by using our strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasible evaluation and control tests of CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Using the CQD-SiNP-pAb2 as the signal-generation tag, a sandwich-type immunoassay was developed for the photoluminescence detection of p53 in a microplate. To investigate the feasibility of this method, the fluorescence intensities of monoclonal anti-p53 antibody-coated microplates were monitored after the each-step reaction with target p53 and CQD-SiNP-pAb2, respectively (Fig. 3 A). Almost no fluorescence signals were observed on the antibody-coated microplate before (curve 'a') and after (curve 'b') incubation with 0.05 ng mL−1 p53 (used as an example). After the resulting microplate reacted with CQD-SiNP-pAb2, however, a strong fluorescence intensity was acquired (curve 'c'). Maybe, one can ask whether the strong signal derived from the nonspecific adsorption of the microplate toward CQD-SiNP-pAb2. To elucidate this issue, mAb1-coated microplate was used for direct incubation with CQD-SiNP-pAb2, and the fluorescence intensity was determined. As seen from curve 'd', the signal was almost the same as that of curve 'a', indicating that the increment of the fluorescence intensity was dependent on the specific antigen-antibody reaction. Therefore, our strategy could be utilized for the detection of target p53 by using CQD-SiNP-pAb2 as the tags.

To further highlight the advantages of CQD-SiNP over pure CQD alone, a comparable study was carried out for the detection of 0.05 ng mL−1 p53 (used as an example) by using CQD-SiNP-pAb2 and CQD-pAb2 as the signal tags with the same assay mode, respectively (note: Anti-p53 pAb2 antibody was labeled onto the CQD referring to the literature [43]). As shown in Fig. 3B, the shift in the fluorescence intensity of using CQD-SiNP-pAb2 as the tags was clearly higher than that of using CQD-pAb2 relative to background signal. Such a high fluorescence intensity was ascribed to the fact that the amount of the encapsulated CQDs into the silica nanoparticles were more than that of individual carbon quantum dot. Hence, utilization of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 could be expected to amplify the detectable signal of the photoluminescence immunoassay.

As described above, CQDs were encapsulated into silica nanoparticles. Typically, a low-amount CQD in the nanoparticle is unfavorable for the generation of strong fluorescence intensity. In contrast, a high-concentration CQDs decreases the fluorescence signal because the water-soluble carbon quantum dots can not be firmly encapsulated into silica nanospheres, thus resulting in the leakage of CQDs. Fig. 3C shows the effect of CQD amount on the fluorescence intensity of the immunoassay by using 0.05 ng mL−1 p53 as an example. The fluorescence intensity initially increased from 10 to 50 mg, and then decreased. An optimum fluorescence intensity was obtained at 50-mg CQDs. Thus, 50 mg of CQDs was used for the preparation of CQD-SiNP in 30-mL AOT isooctane (0.1 M) suspension including 2.5-mL TEOS.

Generally, it takes some time to execute the antigen-antibody reaction. In this study, the detection antibody (pAb2) was labeled on the CQD-SiNP. The used amount of pAb2 antibody for the labeling of CQD-SiNP would directly affect the analytical properties of the immunoassay due to different capture efficiencies. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing volume of pAb2 antibody, and tended to level off after 100 μL (1.0 mg mL−1). Too many pAb2 antibodies did not cause significant increase in the signal. To save the assay cost, 100 μL of pAb2 antibody (1.0 mg mL−1) was added into the CQD-SiNP aqueous solution (1.0 mL, 10 mg mL−1) for the preparation of CQD-SiNP-pAb2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration plots of photoluminescence immunoassay toward p53 standards
ce:section.ce:para: Under the above-optimized conditions, the as-prepared CQD-SiNP-pAb2 was employed for the photoluminescence detection of target p53 with a sandwich-type immunoassay format in anti-p53 capture antibody-coated microplate. Fig. 4 A displays the fluorescence curves of the immunoassay relative to different-concentration p53 standards. The fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing p53 concentration in the sample. A good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of p53 level was acquired within the dynamic working range from 0.01 ng mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1 (Fig. 4A, inset). The linear regression equation could be fitted to FI (a.u.) = 221.75 + 44.35 × log C [p53] (ng mL−1) with a detection limit (LOD) of 2.7 pg mL−1 at a signal to noise ratio of 3S B (where s B is the standard deviation of the blank, n = 11) (r = 0.9961). Each data point represented the average value obtained from three different measurements. The maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) was 9.4%. Further, we studied the batch-to-batch reproducibility of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 toward three concentrations (low-middle-high) including 0.1, 5.0 and 50 ng mL−1 p53, and the RSDs were 12.7%, 9.6% and 11.8% (n = 3), respectively. These results suggested a good reproducibility and precision of the immunoassay. Inspiringly, the LOD of our strategy was comparable with existed commercialized p53 ELISA kits [e.g., human p53 ELISA kit (ab171571) from Abcam (65 pg mL−1; 0.23–15 ng mL−1), mouse p53 ELISA kit (ab224878) from Abcam (44 pg mL−1; 0.1563–10 ng mL−1), human p53 ELISA kit (ab205712) from Abcam (1.0 μg mL−1) and human p53 ELISA kit (RAB0500) from Sigma-Aldrich (0.2 ng mL−1)] and other detection schemes (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of CQD-SiNP-pAb2-based photoluminescence immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: As a newly developed immunoassay, the specificity and selectivity of this system is important toward target analyte. To investigate this issue, several non-targets, which are possibly present in human serum, are determined by using the photoluminescence immunoassay, e.g., neuron-specific enolase (NSE), lactate acid dehydrogenase (LAD), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), cancer antigen 125 (CA 125), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA). As indicated from Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensities for these non-targets alone were almost the same as that of control test, and a strong fluorescence signal was only obtained toward target p53. Significantly, coexistence of non-targets with target p53 did not cause significant change in the fluorescence intensity. Hence, CQD-SiNP-pAb2-based photoluminescence immunoassay had good specificity.

In addition to specificity and reproducibility, the long-time stability of CQD-SiNP-pAb2 is also crucial for the storage of this system. In this regard, the as-prepared CQD-SiNP-pAb2 with the same batch was studied by using them for detection of 1.0 ng mL−1 p53 intermittently. They were stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C when not in use. As shown in Fig. 4C, more than 90% in the fluorescence intensity of the initial signal could be maintained within a 270-day storage period. So, the prepared CQD-SiNP-pAb2 could be used for photoluminescence detection of target p52 during a six-month period without the significant loss of the detectable signal.

To consider the application of this detection system, the accuracy of the photoluminescence immunoassay method was investigated to determine human serum samples. To this end, we collected 12 human serum specimens from the patients of our hospital containing target p53 with the unknown concentrations according to the rules of the local ethical committee (note: Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants including in this study). During the measurement, all handling and processing were performed carefully, and all tools in contact with patient specimens and reagents were disinfected after use. The levels of target p53 in these samples obtained by photoluminescence immunoassay were calculated on the basis of the linear regression equation in Fig. 4A. As the reference, these samples were also assayed by using commercial human p53 ELISA kit. The results are listed in Table 2 . Statistical comparison between two methods was performed by using a t-test for comparison of means preceded by the application of an F-test. The t statistics for each sample was calculated by using independent two-sample t-test with equal sample sizes and equal variance as follows [49]:
t
=
X ¯
1
−
X ¯
2
S
X
1
X
2
⋅
2
n
Where
S
X
1
X
2
=
1 2
(
S
X
1
2
+
S
X
2
2
)
Here
X ¯
1
and
S
X ¯
stand for the mean value of each sample estimated with the immunoassay and the standard error of the mean, respectively (1 = group one, 2 = group two), and n stands for the assay times for each sample. No significant differences were encountered between the two methods at the 0.05 significance level (Table 2) because the t exp were in all cases below than t crit (t crit[4,0.05] = 2.77). Therefore, CQD-SiNP-based photoluminescence immunoassay could be applied for the quantitative screening of target p53 in clinic diagnostics.

In summary, this contribution introduces a simple and low-cost photoluminescence immunoassay for the quantitative monitoring of p53 by using CQD-SiNP as the signal-generation tag. The water soluble carbon quantum dots were extracted from the extensive grapefruit peel. Relative to carbon quantum dots alone, introduction of silica nanoparticles involving the encapsulated CQDs amplified the detectable signal. Moreover, semiconductor silica nanoparticles did not quench the fluorescence signal of the encapsulated carbon quantum dot. Comparison with molecular tag such as fluorophore, CQD-doped silica nanoparticles possess several advantages, i.e., they have high fluorescence intensity, good photostability (thanks to the exclusion of oxygen by the silica encapsulation), and good potential for surface modification with different biomolecules. Due to the merits, our strategy can be easily established by the public for the quantitative monitoring of other proteins or cancer biomarkers by controlling the corresponding antibodies in clinical diagnostics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Startup Fund for Scientific Research, Fujian Medical University (Grant no.: 2017XQ1064), Training Project for Scientific Research Talents, Fujian Provincial Health and Family Planning Commission (Grant no.: 2018-1-53) and Research Project on Education and Teaching Reform, Fujian Medical University (Grant no.: Y17007).
